definitions: A theory suggesting that government financing decisions (debt versus taxes) have no impact on aggregate demand or national saving, as rational consumers anticipate future tax liabilities.
The proposition that, under certain conditions, a government's choice between financing expenditure by debt or by taxes is irrelevant to the economy's real variables.
examples: Many economists debate the real-world applicability of the Ricardian equivalence theorem.
The Ricardian equivalence theorem assumes perfect capital markets and rational expectations.
Critics of the Ricardian equivalence theorem point to liquidity constraints.
